one_line_takeaway: A single, semantically-clean malicious tool document optimized in two phases (retrieval + selection) can hijack no-box LLM-agent tool selection with 88-97% success on GPT-4o while evading StruQ, SecAlign, and perplexity/known-answer detection.
key_finding: ToolHijacker achieves high end-to-end attack success even when the shadow LLM differs architecturally from the target LLM, and substantially outperforms all seven baselines. On GPT-4o the gradient-free attack reaches 96.7% ASR on MetaTool and 88.2% on ToolBench (gradient-based: 92.2% / 83.9%), versus the strongest baseline PoisonedRAG at only 39.3% / 58.3%. The retrieval phase is near-perfect: AHR of 96.1%-100% even against ToolBench's 9,650-document library while injecting only one malicious document. Evaluated defenses are insufficient: fine-tuning defenses StruQ and SecAlign are bypassed (gradient-free ASR 99.6% under StruQ on MetaTool; SecAlign ASR still 84.6%-97.5%), and detection defenses largely fail because the crafted description contains no jarring task-irrelevant instructions (known-answer detection and DataSentinel FNR >90%; PPL/PPL-W miss most, AUC 0.64 and 0.74; PPL-W does catch more of the lower-readability gradient-based attack, e.g. 50% FNR on MetaTool).
core_contribution: The paper introduces ToolHijacker, which the authors present as the first prompt injection attack specifically targeting the tool-selection stage of LLM agents in a no-box scenario. The attacker injects a single crafted malicious tool document into the agent's tool library so the agent consistently selects the attacker's tool for an attacker-chosen target task. The authors formulate malicious-document crafting as an optimization problem spanning both phases of the standard two-step (retrieve then select) tool-selection pipeline, and solve it with a two-phase strategy that splits the tool description into two concatenated subsequences R (optimized for retrieval) and S (optimized for selection). They provide both a gradient-free and a gradient-based variant of the attack. This is the paper's OWN contribution; prior attacks (JudgeDeceiver, PoisonedRAG, manual injections) are cited baselines that the authors argue are incomplete because they address only the selection phase (manual attacks, JudgeDeceiver) or optimize for a single query and target generation rather than tool selection (PoisonedRAG).
threat_model: No-box attacker who knows the target task conceptually but cannot access: (1) the target task descriptions Q, (2) the contents of other tool documents in the library, (3) the retrieval parameter k or the top-k retrieved set, or (4) the parameters of, or query access to, the target retriever and target LLM. The attacker's capability is to publish a malicious tool (name + description + API spec) to an open third-party tool hub such as Hugging Face Hub, Apify, or PulseMCP that feeds into agent tool libraries, and to build a local shadow pipeline (shadow task descriptions Q' disjoint from Q, shadow tool documents D', shadow retriever, shadow LLM) to design and validate the attack. Because agents follow a select-and-execute model, a selected malicious tool runs without further verification, enabling data exfiltration, unauthorized actions, or privacy breaches. The authors cite real MCP tool-poisoning incidents as evidence the capability is realistic.
limitations: Attack scope is limited to tool SELECTION; tool calling/execution is out of scope, and joint attacks on both are left as future work. Effectiveness depends on constructing a good shadow pipeline and, especially for the gradient-based variant, a sufficiently capable shadow LLM (gradient-based ASR is shadow-LLM-sensitive, dropping to 34% on Llama-3-70B when Llama-2-7B is the shadow LLM; gradient-free is more robust, minimum 70% on Claude-3-Haiku). ASR is consistently lower on ToolBench than MetaTool, attributed to the larger tool library. The gradient-based attack's token-level optimization can reduce readability, making it the one attack that PPL-W partially detects. ASR also degrades at very small k and small k' (e.g. k=1 gives 89% AHR/ASR for gradient-free). Ethics: experiments were in controlled local environments with no real tool deployment, and OpenAI, Anthropic, and LangChain were notified.

datasets_used: MetaTool (21,127 instances, 199 benign tool documents from OpenAI Plugins; 10 target tasks, 1,000 target task descriptions)
ToolBench (126,486 instruction-tuning samples; 9,650 benign tool documents from RapidAPI after dedup; 10 target tasks, 1,000 target task descriptions)
quantitative_results: Attack Hit Rate (retrieval): gradient-free 99.9% / gradient-based 100% on MetaTool; 96.1% / 97.8% on ToolBench (single injected document)
Claude-3-Haiku least sensitive but still >=70% ASR
Detection FNR: known-answer detection 100%, DataSentinel 100% (gradient-free) / >=80% (gradient-based); PPL misses ~90% of gradient-based docs at <1% FPR; PPL/PPL-W AUC 0.64 and 0.74
Ablation: removing S drops gradient-free ASR from 99% to 5%; removing R drops gradient-free AHR from 100% to 65%
Gradient-based ASR rises from 32% (1 shadow task description) to 98% (7); gradient-free stays >=92% with a single shadow description
baselines_compared: Naive attack (6.0% MetaTool / 24.8% ToolBench ASR on GPT-4o)
